summary: I was involved with the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, or SNCC, during the early 1960s. In 1961, SNCC was transitioning from a student coordinating group to one with full-time workers, and we were deeply engaged in civil rights activities in Mississippi. There were intense debates within SNCC about whether to focus on voter registration or direct action, with leaders like Bob Moses, Chuck, Charles Jones, Tom Jenkins, Ella Baker, Diane Nash, and myself all playing roles. SNCC was central to organizing grassroots movements and direct actions like sit-ins and voter registration drives across Mississippi.

In 1961, the Freedom Rides were a major event that many of us in SNCC participated in, either by riding or organizing. I was in Jackson, Mississippi, helping to organize local people for the rides. These rides were part of the broader civil rights struggle to challenge segregation in the South, and they overlapped with other efforts like voter registration and direct action campaigns. The Freedom Rides helped galvanize local activism and brought national attention to Mississippi's racial injustices.

Mississippi was the central battleground for much of our civil rights work in the early 1960s. In 1961 and 1962, we were organizing voter registration drives and direct action campaigns across the state, including in places like McComb, Amite County, and Jackson. The state was known for its violent resistance to black voting rights, which made our work dangerous but crucial. Leaders like Bob Moses traveled extensively throughout Mississippi to build grassroots movements and develop new strategies for change.

Bob Moses was a key figure in our movement in Mississippi starting in the spring of 1961. He traveled around the state, especially to places like McComb and Amite County, talking with local activists such as Allie Mone and Curtis Bryant. Bob was instrumental in shaping our strategies, especially the idea of building grassroots voter registration and the concept of a parallel political structure. He was a visionary leader whose ideas influenced much of SNCC's work in Mississippi.

Allie Mone was one of the local activists in McComb, Mississippi, whom Bob Moses met with during his early travels in 1961. Though I’m not sure if Bob spoke to Steptoe first, he definitely talked to Curtis Bryant and then Allie Mone in McComb. These local leaders were crucial in connecting SNCC organizers with the community and helping to build the grassroots movement. Their involvement helped lay the groundwork for voter registration and direct action campaigns in the area.

Curtis Bryant was another important local figure in McComb, Mississippi, who Bob Moses met with in the spring of 1961. He was part of the early discussions that helped SNCC understand the local context and organize voter registration efforts. Along with others, Curtis helped us mobilize people for sit-ins and other direct actions in the fall of 1961. His role was vital in bridging the work of outside organizers with the community’s needs.

McComb was a focal point for much of our civil rights organizing starting in 1961. Bob Moses, Curtis Bryant, and Allie Mone were active there, and it was the site of early voter registration drives and direct action campaigns like lunch counter sit-ins. The community faced severe backlash, including beatings and the expulsion of 114 students from school due to their activism. We also formed the McComb Freedom Movement there, which helped coordinate local efforts and sustain the struggle.

Amite County was one of the counties near McComb where civil rights activities spread in 1961 and 1962. Bob Moses traveled there, and local activists like Steptoe were involved in voter registration efforts. The movement was still in its early stages, with grassroots attempts to get people to register to vote despite significant resistance. Amite County was part of the broader regional effort in southwest Mississippi to challenge segregation and disenfranchisement.

Janice Pemares was one of the few full-time workers in SNCC before the spring of 1961. At that time, SNCC was mostly a student coordinating committee without many full-time staff, and Janice was among the earliest to take on that role. Her work helped lay the foundation for the transition to a more organized and professional movement. Although I’m not sure of all her specific activities, she was part of the early SNCC team during this critical period.

Ed King was involved with SNCC but was not yet working full-time by the spring of 1961. While Janice Pemares was one of the few workers then, Ed King came on board later. His involvement grew as SNCC expanded its activities in Mississippi and beyond. Though he wasn’t active in the earliest voter registration or direct action campaigns, he became part of the broader movement during its development.

Chuck was one of the key SNCC leaders involved in debates during the summer of 1961 about whether to focus on voter registration or direct action. Alongside Bob Moses, Charles Jones, Tom Jenkins, Ella Baker, Diane Nash, and others, Chuck helped shape the direction of the movement. He was a strong advocate for voter registration efforts and was involved in organizing campaigns in Mississippi. His leadership was important in balancing the different strategies within SNCC.

Charles Jones was deeply involved in SNCC’s work in Mississippi and was a major proponent of voter registration during the debates in 1961. He was designated as the director of voter registration efforts in Mississippi, working alongside Diane Nash, who directed direct action. Charles was present during key meetings and helped organize campaigns in places like McComb. His efforts contributed significantly to expanding the grassroots political movement in the state.

Tom Jenkins was another SNCC leader active in the summer of 1961 debates about voter registration versus direct action. He was one of the strongest advocates for getting involved in voter registration work in Mississippi. Tom worked closely with others like Charles Jones and Bob Moses to organize grassroots campaigns. His role helped push SNCC toward a dual strategy of direct action and political organizing.

Ella Baker was a respected elder and leader within SNCC who participated in the intense debates in 1961 about the movement’s direction. She was part of the leadership group that included Bob Moses, Chuck, Charles Jones, Diane Nash, and others. Ella’s experience and guidance helped shape the strategies of both direct action and voter registration. Her influence was crucial in maintaining a focus on grassroots organizing and empowering local communities in Mississippi.

Diane Nash was the director of direct action for SNCC in Mississippi and a key figure in the 1961 debates over strategy. She advocated for a campaign called 'Move on Mississippi,' which aimed to organize thousands of people to challenge segregation through direct action. Diane worked alongside Charles Jones, who directed voter registration, and their efforts complemented each other. Her leadership helped energize public accommodations protests and broader civil rights activism in the state.

Reggie Robertson was one of the SNCC workers involved in voter registration efforts in Mississippi in 1961. Along with Bob Moses and John Hardy, Reggie worked in McComb and surrounding areas to encourage local people to register to vote. His participation was part of the early grassroots movement that sought to empower black Mississippians despite violent opposition. Reggie’s work helped lay the foundation for the broader voter registration campaigns that followed.

John Hardy was active in voter registration work in Mississippi alongside Bob Moses and Reggie Robertson starting in 1961. He worked in areas like McComb and the Delta, including Cleveland, where he connected with local activists like Amite Moore. John was part of the early efforts to get working-class people, sharecroppers, and farmers to attempt to register to vote. His work contributed to spreading the movement across multiple counties in Mississippi.

Liberty, Mississippi, was one of the places where I went to the courthouse to support voter registration efforts in 1961. It was part of the broader grassroots campaign to encourage local black citizens to attempt to register despite the risks involved. Activities in Liberty were connected to the larger movement in southwest Mississippi, including McComb and Amite County. The work there was dangerous but essential for building political power in the state.

Hollis Watkins was one of the young activists involved in direct action campaigns in Mississippi in 1961. Along with Curtis Hayes and Brenda Travis, he participated in sit-ins at lunch counters in McComb. These actions helped galvanize the community and supported voter registration efforts by drawing attention to segregation and injustice. Hollis was part of the emerging grassroots leadership in the state’s civil rights movement.

Brenda Travis was a young activist who took part in the lunch counter sit-ins in McComb, Mississippi, in 1961. Alongside Hollis Watkins and Curtis Hayes, she helped spark direct action protests that energized the local civil rights movement. Brenda’s participation led to severe consequences, including school expulsions of many students involved in activism. Her courage was emblematic of the youth leadership that was critical to the movement’s growth.

The McComb Freedom Movement was an organization formed around 1961 to coordinate civil rights activities in McComb, Mississippi. We created it to unify voter registration efforts and direct action campaigns like sit-ins and protests. The movement faced intense backlash, including beatings and the expulsion of over a hundred students from school. Despite this, the McComb Freedom Movement was a crucial local force in sustaining the struggle for voting rights and desegregation.

Tom Hayden was an outside observer and writer who documented much of the civil rights movement, including the activities in Mississippi. I have his book, which helped me recall some details about the McComb Freedom Movement and the broader organizing efforts. While he wasn’t directly involved in the day-to-day work, his writings provide valuable insights into the debates and strategies we faced. His documentation helped preserve the history of our struggle.

Jackson was a major hub for SNCC organizing and civil rights activities in 1961 and beyond. I was based there for much of the time, helping to organize local people for the Freedom Rides and voter registration efforts. We held meetings there to debate strategy and plan actions, including the big meeting in September 1961 that clarified our approach to direct action and political work. Jackson was a critical city for coordinating statewide efforts in Mississippi.

Move on Mississippi was a campaign proposed by Diane Nash in 1961 aimed at organizing thousands of people to engage in direct action throughout the state. The idea was to raise hell, as Diane put it, by challenging segregation in public accommodations and other areas. This campaign was part of the broader debate within SNCC about balancing voter registration with direct action. Although it was ambitious, Move on Mississippi helped sharpen our vision and energize activists during that period.
